title: Ischaemic Colitis
oneLiner: Reduced blood flow to the colon causing inflammation/infarction; typically in elderly with vascular risk factors; often mimics surgical emergencies.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute mesenteric ischaemia (superior mesenteric artery occlusion):** diffuse abdominal pain out of proportion to exam + lactic acidosis → needs urgent CT angiography and surgical embolectomy
- **Colonic perforation:** peritonism (rigidity, rebound, involuntary guarding) + free air on AXR/CT → immediate surgical review
- **Septic shock from bowel infarction:** hypotension + rising lactate despite fluids → emergent ICU referral and broad-spectrum antibiotics while awaiting imaging
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute onset left lower quadrant or supra-pubic pain (cramping then constant)
  - Rectal bleeding often within 12–24 hours (dark red or bright red)
  - Diarrhoea (bloody, frequent, small volume)
  - Nausea/vomiting in 20–30%
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Localised tenderness over affected colon (left iliac fossa most common)
  - Absence of peritoneal signs early (if present → indicate infarction/perforation)
  - Often low-grade fever, tachycardia
  - ± hypovolaemic signs (if significant blood loss)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - *Ischaemic colitis* – elderly, vascular risk, pain before bleeding
  - *Diverticulitis* – left iliac fossa pain, fever, but absence of rectal bleeding early
  - *Infectious colitis (Salmonella, Campylobacter)* – contact history, diarrhoea precedes pain, fever high
  - *Pseudomembranous colitis (C. diff)* – antibiotic history, watery diarrhoea, leucocytosis
  - *Inflammatory bowel disease (UC/Crohn's)* – chronic relapsing history, extra-intestinal features, younger age
  - *Colorectal carcinoma* – weight loss, change in bowel habit, ?obstruction
- **Splitter:** “Bloody diarrhoea with pain starting before bleeding → ischaemic colitis; pain after bleeding → infectious or inflammatory colitis”
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Digital rectal exam (presence/colour of blood, masses, tenderness)
  - Observations (NEWS2) – hypotension and tachycardia worsen ischaemia
  - 12-lead ECG (exclude atrial fibrillation as embolic source)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb drop, WCC elevated), U&E, creatinine, CRP
  - Lactate (if >2 mmol/L suggests bowel ischaemia)
  - D-dimer (non-specific but often raised)
  - Troponin (exclude concurrent MI as cause)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (portal venous phase) – gold standard; shows segmental colon wall thickening, thumbprinting, pneumatosis, or perforation
  - **Second-line:** CT angiography (if suspect superior mesenteric artery occlusion)
  - **Avoid:** colonoscopy in acute setting (risk of perforation; only if diagnosis unclear or to rule out other causes after 48-72h)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single validated score; classic triad of acute left-sided pain + bleeding + CT findings of segmental colitis (right-sided involvement suggests mesenteric ischaemia)
- **Never-miss test:** CT abdomen with IV contrast (differentiates ischaemic colitis from acute mesenteric occlusion and perforation)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Fluid resuscitation (crystalloid, e.g. Hartmann's or 0.9% saline, 500 mL bolus then reassess)
  - NBM (nil by mouth)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g IV (avoid NSAIDs – worsen renal perfusion)
  - Stop anticoagulants/antiplatelets if safe; discuss with haematology if high thrombotic risk
  - Broad-spectrum antibiotics (if peritonitis or high suspicion of infarction) – e.g. co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or piperacillin-tazobactam [check local policy]
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Serial abdominal exams (q4-6h) – pain, guarding, distension
  - Monitor NEWS2, urine output, lactate (repeat 6-12 hourly)
  - Maintain mean arterial pressure (MAP) >65 mmHg – hypotension worsens ischaemia
  - Correct anaemia if Hb <70 g/L (transfuse 1-2 units)
  - If no improvement or worsening peritonism → urgent surgical review for possible laparotomy/colonic resection
  - Consider therapeutic anticoagulation for embolic source (AF) after acute phase (discuss with cardiology)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Treat modifiable risk factors: control hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia
  - Consider smoking cessation, antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 75mg daily) for secondary prevention in elderly with atherosclerosis (after GI risk-benefit assessment)
  - Colonoscopy 6–12 weeks after recovery to evaluate for underlying stricture or malignancy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid at 125 mL/hr (adjust for renal function, output, lactate)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless active bleeding or haemoglobin unstable
  - **Diet:** NBM initially; reintroduce clear fluids when pain settling and no vomiting
  - **Observations:** 4 hourly (NEWS2, abdominal girth); if NEWS2 ≥5 → escalate
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS for 7 days (if infection suspected or peritonitis) – [check local policy for exact dose]
  - Metronidazole 500mg IV TDS if penicillin allergy
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS (if severe sepsis) – [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: caution in hepatic impairment, reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min
  - NSAIDs: avoid in any acute gastrointestinal condition
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav dose adjustment in renal impairment: CrCl 10–30 mL/min → 1.2g IV every 12 hours; <10 mL/min → 1.2g IV every 24 hours
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Over-rapid fluid administration in elderly with heart failure or renal impairment → pulmonary oedema; use cautious boluses (250 mL) and reassess
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Bowel infarction → peritonitis, sepsis
  - Colonic perforation → emergency laparotomy
  - Toxic megacolon (rare) → surgical resection
  - Acute kidney injury from hypoperfusion
- **Chronic:**
  - Colonic stricture (3–5 weeks post-event) – may cause obstruction
  - Recurrent ischaemic colitis (if underlying aetiology untreated)
  - Protein-losing enteropathy (rare)
sections.Prognosis: - Overall mortality ~5–10% (higher if surgery needed, 30–40%); majority recover with supportive care and no recurrence if risk factors controlled; right-sided ischaemia has worse prognosis (higher infarction rate)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check lactate and CT abdomen** before calling surgical reg – high lactate with CT changes warrants urgent review
- **Maintain MAP >65 mmHg** – vasopressors (e.g. noradrenaline) if fluid-resistant hypotension; this is more important than “chasing” mild anaemia
- **Consider atrial fibrillation** as embolic source – ECG and check rhythm; anticoagulate after acute phase (day 3-5) if no ongoing bleeding
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give steroids** – they are ineffective in ischaemic colitis and increase risk of perforation
- **Do not use antimotility agents (loperamide)** – they worsen toxin retention and can precipitate toxic megacolon
- **Do not delay surgical consultation** if peritonitis or clinical deterioration despite fluid resuscitation – earlier resection reduces mortality
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening abdominal pain, new fever, inability to tolerate oral fluids, large volume rectal bleeding, or passing only flatus (possible stricture/obstruction)
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 1 week; urgent outpatient surgical/gastroenterology clinic in 2-3 weeks; colonoscopy in 6-12 weeks (to assess for ischaemic stricture or underlying malignancy)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU when:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising trend)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L and not falling after initial fluids
  - GCS <15 or shock (systolic BP <90 mmHg)
  - Signs of peritonitis (guarding, rigidity, rebound) → immediate surgical review
- **Threshold for urgent CT:** any patient with severe pain + lactate >2 + co-morbidities; NEWS2 ≥3 plus abnormal abdominal exam

rawMarkdown:
# Ischaemic Colitis  
**One-liner:** Reduced blood flow to the colon causing inflammation/infarction; typically in elderly with vascular risk factors; often mimics surgical emergencies.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute mesenteric ischaemia (superior mesenteric artery occlusion):** diffuse abdominal pain out of proportion to exam + lactic acidosis → needs urgent CT angiography and surgical embolectomy
- **Colonic perforation:** peritonism (rigidity, rebound, involuntary guarding) + free air on AXR/CT → immediate surgical review
- **Septic shock from bowel infarction:** hypotension + rising lactate despite fluids → emergent ICU referral and broad-spectrum antibiotics while awaiting imaging

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute onset left lower quadrant or supra-pubic pain (cramping then constant)
  - Rectal bleeding often within 12–24 hours (dark red or bright red)
  - Diarrhoea (bloody, frequent, small volume)
  - Nausea/vomiting in 20–30%
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Localised tenderness over affected colon (left iliac fossa most common)
  - Absence of peritoneal signs early (if present → indicate infarction/perforation)
  - Often low-grade fever, tachycardia
  - ± hypovolaemic signs (if significant blood loss)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - *Ischaemic colitis* – elderly, vascular risk, pain before bleeding
  - *Diverticulitis* – left iliac fossa pain, fever, but absence of rectal bleeding early
  - *Infectious colitis (Salmonella, Campylobacter)* – contact history, diarrhoea precedes pain, fever high
  - *Pseudomembranous colitis (C. diff)* – antibiotic history, watery diarrhoea, leucocytosis
  - *Inflammatory bowel disease (UC/Crohn's)* – chronic relapsing history, extra-intestinal features, younger age
  - *Colorectal carcinoma* – weight loss, change in bowel habit, ?obstruction
- **Splitter:** “Bloody diarrhoea with pain starting before bleeding → ischaemic colitis; pain after bleeding → infectious or inflammatory colitis”

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Digital rectal exam (presence/colour of blood, masses, tenderness)
  - Observations (NEWS2) – hypotension and tachycardia worsen ischaemia
  - 12-lead ECG (exclude atrial fibrillation as embolic source)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb drop, WCC elevated), U&E, creatinine, CRP
  - Lactate (if >2 mmol/L suggests bowel ischaemia)
  - D-dimer (non-specific but often raised)
  - Troponin (exclude concurrent MI as cause)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (portal venous phase) – gold standard; shows segmental colon wall thickening, thumbprinting, pneumatosis, or perforation
  - **Second-line:** CT angiography (if suspect superior mesenteric artery occlusion)
  - **Avoid:** colonoscopy in acute setting (risk of perforation; only if diagnosis unclear or to rule out other causes after 48-72h)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single validated score; classic triad of acute left-sided pain + bleeding + CT findings of segmental colitis (right-sided involvement suggests mesenteric ischaemia)
- **Never-miss test:** CT abdomen with IV contrast (differentiates ischaemic colitis from acute mesenteric occlusion and perforation)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Fluid resuscitation (crystalloid, e.g. Hartmann's or 0.9% saline, 500 mL bolus then reassess)
  - NBM (nil by mouth)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g IV (avoid NSAIDs – worsen renal perfusion)
  - Stop anticoagulants/antiplatelets if safe; discuss with haematology if high thrombotic risk
  - Broad-spectrum antibiotics (if peritonitis or high suspicion of infarction) – e.g. co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or piperacillin-tazobactam [check local policy]
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Serial abdominal exams (q4-6h) – pain, guarding, distension
  - Monitor NEWS2, urine output, lactate (repeat 6-12 hourly)
  - Maintain mean arterial pressure (MAP) >65 mmHg – hypotension worsens ischaemia
  - Correct anaemia if Hb <70 g/L (transfuse 1-2 units)
  - If no improvement or worsening peritonism → urgent surgical review for possible laparotomy/colonic resection
  - Consider therapeutic anticoagulation for embolic source (AF) after acute phase (discuss with cardiology)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Treat modifiable risk factors: control hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia
  - Consider smoking cessation, antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 75mg daily) for secondary prevention in elderly with atherosclerosis (after GI risk-benefit assessment)
  - Colonoscopy 6–12 weeks after recovery to evaluate for underlying stricture or malignancy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid at 125 mL/hr (adjust for renal function, output, lactate)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless active bleeding or haemoglobin unstable
  - **Diet:** NBM initially; reintroduce clear fluids when pain settling and no vomiting
  - **Observations:** 4 hourly (NEWS2, abdominal girth); if NEWS2 ≥5 → escalate

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS for 7 days (if infection suspected or peritonitis) – [check local policy for exact dose]
  - Metronidazole 500mg IV TDS if penicillin allergy
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS (if severe sepsis) – [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: caution in hepatic impairment, reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min
  - NSAIDs: avoid in any acute gastrointestinal condition
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav dose adjustment in renal impairment: CrCl 10–30 mL/min → 1.2g IV every 12 hours; <10 mL/min → 1.2g IV every 24 hours
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Over-rapid fluid administration in elderly with heart failure or renal impairment → pulmonary oedema; use cautious boluses (250 mL) and reassess

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Bowel infarction → peritonitis, sepsis
  - Colonic perforation → emergency laparotomy
  - Toxic megacolon (rare) → surgical resection
  - Acute kidney injury from hypoperfusion
- **Chronic:**
  - Colonic stricture (3–5 weeks post-event) – may cause obstruction
  - Recurrent ischaemic colitis (if underlying aetiology untreated)
  - Protein-losing enteropathy (rare)

### Prognosis
- Overall mortality ~5–10% (higher if surgery needed, 30–40%); majority recover with supportive care and no recurrence if risk factors controlled; right-sided ischaemia has worse prognosis (higher infarction rate)

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check lactate and CT abdomen** before calling surgical reg – high lactate with CT changes warrants urgent review
- **Maintain MAP >65 mmHg** – vasopressors (e.g. noradrenaline) if fluid-resistant hypotension; this is more important than “chasing” mild anaemia
- **Consider atrial fibrillation** as embolic source – ECG and check rhythm; anticoagulate after acute phase (day 3-5) if no ongoing bleeding

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give steroids** – they are ineffective in ischaemic colitis and increase risk of perforation
- **Do not use antimotility agents (loperamide)** – they worsen toxin retention and can precipitate toxic megacolon
- **Do not delay surgical consultation** if peritonitis or clinical deterioration despite fluid resuscitation – earlier resection reduces mortality

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening abdominal pain, new fever, inability to tolerate oral fluids, large volume rectal bleeding, or passing only flatus (possible stricture/obstruction)
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 1 week; urgent outpatient surgical/gastroenterology clinic in 2-3 weeks; colonoscopy in 6-12 weeks (to assess for ischaemic stricture or underlying malignancy)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU when:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising trend)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L and not falling after initial fluids
  - GCS <15 or shock (systolic BP <90 mmHg)
  - Signs of peritonitis (guarding, rigidity, rebound) → immediate surgical review
- **Threshold for urgent CT:** any patient with severe pain + lactate >2 + co-morbidities; NEWS2 ≥3 plus abnormal abdominal exam
